questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why is liquid water denser than steam?
options: The particles in steam are heavier than the particles in water.
The liquid particles stop moving completely.
Steam contains no energy so it spreads out.
The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.
questions.senNote: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a clinic worksheet, a learner checks: a student compares the same mass of water as liquid and gas. Why is steam less dense?
options: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a museum label, a visitor reads: a results table shows particles in steam spread far apart. What does this mean for density?
options: Particles shrink to make steam dense.
The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a school lab card, a pupil answers: why does the same mass of steam occupy a larger volume than liquid water?
options: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a factory safety note, an apprentice reviews: a student says steam particles are further apart than liquid particles. How does this affect density?
options: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a space centre display, a guide explains: which particle explanation fits liquid water having a higher density than steam?
options: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
Particles shrink to make steam dense.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a revision flashcard, a student practises: liquid water is denser than steam. What does the particle model say?
options: The particles are closer together in the liquid, so the same mass takes up less volume.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are closer together in the liquid, so the same mass takes up less volume.
questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.subject: Physics

options: Steam is less dense because its particles are more spread out.
questions.correctAnswer: Steam is less dense because its particles are more spread out.
questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a observatory data sheet, an astronomer reviews: a whiteboard shows particles in steam spread far apart. What does this mean for density?
options: The steam must occupy a larger volume, so its density is lower.
Particles shrink to make steam dense.
questions.correctAnswer: The steam must occupy a larger volume, so its density is lower.
questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.
questions.senNote: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.explanation: Steam is less dense because its particles are much further apart than in liquid water.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A block has a mass of 40 g and a volume of 6 cm^3. What is its density?
questions.explanation: Density equals mass divided by volume.
questions.senNote: Density equals mass divided by volume.

questions.senNote: Density is mass divided by volume, giving 7.14 g/cm^3.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best describes internal energy?
options: The particles become a different element.
It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a clinic worksheet, a learner checks: a student heats a block and asks what energy store has increased. Which definition is correct?
options: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
The substance loses all potential energy.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a museum label, a visitor reads: a results table says internal energy depends on particles. What does it include?
options: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
The particles become fixed in a perfect lattice.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a school lab card, a pupil answers: which statement describes the internal energy of a system?
options: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
Heating removes kinetic energy from the particles.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.

It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.

questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.

questions.subject: Physics

options: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
The temperature falls whenever energy is transferred in.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.
questions.senNote: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.

questions.correctAnswer: The hot spoon has more internal energy because its particles have more kinetic energy.
questions.explanation: Internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of particles in a system.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A sample of ice has a mass of 0.3 kg. The specific latent heat of fusion is 334000 J/kg. How much energy is needed to melt it?

questions.senNote: Energy transferred for a change of state equals mass multiplied by specific latent heat.

questions.explanation: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.
questions.senNote: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a workshop checklist, an engineer checks: a substance is boiling at a constant temperature. What is the energy used for?
options: Specific heat capacity is energy needed to change temperature; specific latent heat is energy needed to change state without changing temperature.
It lowers the melting point to zero.
questions.correctAnswer: Specific heat capacity is energy needed to change temperature; specific latent heat is energy needed to change state without changing temperature.
questions.explanation: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.
questions.senNote: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a field trip notebook, a pupil records: which explanation fits latent heat during a change of state?
options: The steam releases energy as it changes state, even though the particles themselves do not change.
questions.correctAnswer: The steam releases energy as it changes state, even though the particles themselves do not change.
questions.explanation: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.
questions.senNote: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a science fair poster, a team explains: a sample melts while its temperature stays steady. Where is the energy going?
options: It would just melt all the ice.
It lowers the melting point to zero.
questions.correctAnswer: It would just melt all the ice.
questions.explanation: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.
questions.senNote: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a exam practice page, a candidate works: a parent heats ice at its melting point. Why does the temperature not rise?
options: Energy is being used to melt the ice rather than raise its temperature.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy is being used to melt the ice rather than raise its temperature.
questions.explanation: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.
questions.senNote: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a home study card, a learner revises: a log book shows a flat section during melting. What is the transferred energy doing?
questions.explanation: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.
questions.senNote: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a research log book, an assistant notes: during a change of state, why can energy be transferred with no temperature increase?
options: Changing liquid water at 100 C into steam at 100 C
questions.correctAnswer: Changing liquid water at 100 C into steam at 100 C
questions.explanation: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.
questions.senNote: Latent heat changes particle arrangement during a change of state.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a planetarium screen, a visitor chooses: a sound desk shows E = mass x latent heat = 200400 J. What is E?
questions.explanation: E = mL gives 200400 J.
questions.senNote: E = mL gives 200400 J.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a radio studio demo, a presenter explains: which value is correct for the latent heat energy calculation result 233800 J?
questions.explanation: E = mL gives 233800 J.
questions.senNote: E = mL gives 233800 J.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a energy survey report, a surveyor writes: a clinic melting calculation gives 66800 J. Which energy is correct?
questions.explanation: E = mL gives 66800 J.
questions.senNote: E = mL gives 66800 J.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about particles in a solid is correct?
options: They are far apart and move freely in all directions.
They have no kinetic energy at all.
They fill the whole container because they can flow.
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.senNote: In a solid, particles are close together and vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a clinic worksheet, a learner checks: a student models a solid with beads. How should the beads move?
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a museum label, a visitor reads: a results table shows particles close together in fixed places. Which state is shown?
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a school lab card, a pupil answers: what is true about the arrangement and motion of particles in a solid?
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a factory safety note, an apprentice reviews: a metal block keeps its shape. Which particle description explains this?
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: On a space centre display, a guide explains: which answer best describes solid particles at room temperature?
questions.correctAnswer: They vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Use particle ideas to describe a solid
questions.question: On a revision flashcard, a student practises: which statement describes particles in a solid?
options: Its particles are close together and only vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.correctAnswer: Its particles are close together and only vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Choose the best explanation for particles in a solid
questions.question: On a hospital training card, a trainee checks: a astronomer models a solid with beads. How should the beads move?
options: They are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.correctAnswer: They are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Compare particle arrangements in different states
questions.question: On a observatory data sheet, an astronomer reviews: a whiteboard shows particles close together in fixed places. Which state is shown?
options: The particles stay the same, but they are able to move past each other more easily.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles stay the same, but they are able to move past each other more easily.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Reject a misconception about particles in solids and liquids
questions.question: On a classroom board, a group discusses: what is true about the arrangement and motion of particles in a solid?
options: The student is wrong because liquid particles can move past each other, even though they stay close together.
questions.correctAnswer: The student is wrong because liquid particles can move past each other, even though they stay close together.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Use particle ideas to explain a solid

options: Its particles only vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.correctAnswer: Its particles only vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

options: The particles are close together and cannot move past each other freely.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are close together and cannot move past each other freely.
questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

options: In both, particles are close together, but only in the liquid can they move past each other.

questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
questions.senNote: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.

options: The particles in the mud can move past each other, but the particles in the brick cannot.

questions.explanation: Particles in a solid are close together and vibrate about fixed positions.
